one_line_takeaway: In a large public competition (464 participants, 271K+ attempts, 8,648 successful dual-objective attacks), every one of 13 frontier/open-weight models proved vulnerable to indirect prompt injections that both execute a harmful agentic action and conceal the compromise in the visible response, with ASR from 0.5% (Claude Opus 4.5) to 8.5% (Gemini 2.5 Pro), robustness tracking model family/training more than raw capability, and universal attack templates (e.g., a 'Holodeck' fake-simulated-environment injection) transferring across up to 21 of 41 behaviors and 9 model families.
key_finding: All 13 models were vulnerable to concealment-satisfying indirect prompt injection; overall ASR ranged from 0.5% (Claude Opus 4.5, 61/11,969 attempts) to 8.5% (Gemini 2.5 Pro, 1,670/19,630 attempts). Anthropic and OpenAI models were the most robust; within the Claude family robustness scaled with capability (Opus 4.5 0.5% < Sonnet 4.5 1.0% < Haiku 4.5 1.3%). By agentic setting, tool-use was most vulnerable overall (4.82% ASR, 95% CI [4.68%,4.96%]), followed by computer-use (3.13%, [2.96%,3.31%]) and coding (2.51%, [2.41%,2.62%]), though Claude Opus 4.5 was an exception (higher computer-use ASR at 1.1% than tool-use at 0.6%). Capability (proxied by GPQA Diamond) correlated only weakly and non-significantly with ASR (r=-0.31, p=0.3); e.g. Gemini 2.5 Pro and Kimi K2 both score ~85% on GPQA Diamond yet differ sharply in ASR (8.5% vs 4.8%), leading the authors to conclude robustness is driven more by model family/training recipe than raw capability. Transfer experiments on the curated 2,679-attack benchmark showed transfer ASR much higher than raw competition ASR (e.g. Qwen3 VL 235B 52.5% [1396/2658], Nova 1 Premier 50.6%, down to Claude Opus 4.5 2.5% [65/2607]) and a strong asymmetry: the 44 attacks that broke Claude Opus 4.5 transferred at 44-81% to every other model, while attacks that broke the most vulnerable models (e.g. Qwen3 VL 235B) transferred poorly upward (0% to Opus 4.5, 1% to Sonnet 4.5). A connected-component analysis over attack-string embeddings identified 5 'universal attack' clusters, the largest ('Holodeck' -- framing the interaction as a simulated environment with a fake meta-level control interface) comprising 63 minimally-varied submissions effective across 21 of 41 behaviors and 9 models. Top individual strategies by ASR (out of 27 total, ranked in Table 3/6) were 'Fake Chain of Thought' (4.3% ASR over 13,878 submissions), 'Request to Disable Critical and Safety-Oriented Thoughts' (4.1% over 13,494), and 'Offer Reward and Punishment' (4.0% over 6,575). Kimi K2 with thinking enabled was more robust in transfer than the non-thinking variant (30% vs 35% transfer ASR).
core_contribution: The paper (Gray Swan AI, in collaboration with Anthropic, Meta, OpenAI (feedback), UK AISI, and US CAISI) reports results from a large-scale public red-teaming competition ('Indirect Prompt Injection Arena') that evaluates indirect prompt injection (IPI) against LLM agents under a novel 'concealment' requirement: a successful attack must both (1) force the agent to execute a harmful action and (2) pass scenario-specific criteria on the agent's user-facing final response (chiefly, concealing that the injection/compromise occurred). The authors curated 41 agentic scenarios across three settings -- tool-use (18), coding (15), and computer-use (8) -- and ran a 3-week, $40k-prize competition that drew 464 unique participants who produced 271,588 deduplicated attack attempts (67,634 submissions) against 13 frontier/open-weight models, yielding 8,648 successful (dual-objective) attacks. They report overall attack success rate (ASR) by model, break ASR down by agentic setting, run cross-model transfer experiments on a curated benchmark of 2,679 successful attacks (across all 13 competition models plus Gemini 3 Pro and non-thinking Kimi K2), cluster attack strategies (27 final strategy categories from an LLM-tagging + embedding-clustering pipeline) and attack strings (5 'universal attack' clusters via cosine-similarity graph/connected components), and open-source the evaluation kit plus a subset of successful attacks against Qwen.
threat_model: Attacker controls a fixed portion of a single external data source (an email, a file/config, a webpage, a PR description, a UI sidebar/ad, etc.) that is the last segment of a prefilled multi-turn agentic transcript; the agent then operates one turn (possibly with multiple tool-call rounds) on that transcript. The attacker sees the full conversation prefill before crafting the injection, knows the exact target harmful tool call/arguments, and can observe which tools the agent used in the prefilled turns (but not full tool definitions). Attacker and agent are limited to a single turn (no multi-turn escalation). The authors explicitly state this is MORE permissive than realistic conditions (real attackers have limited visibility into conversation history/available tools and would need separate reconnaissance), so the reported ASRs are framed as an upper bound on single-turn agent vulnerability, not real-world attack frequency.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) the threat model is more permissive than realistic deployment (attacker has full visibility into conversation and exact target action), so ASRs are an upper bound, not real-world attack frequency; (2) attack effort/attention across models and scenarios was non-uniform (a Pareto-like distribution where a few skilled participants drove disproportionate success), which could bias comparisons, though the authors note no clear correlation between ASR and number of unique attackers per model; (3) each attack was evaluated only once per model (no multi-shot/bootstrap aggregation), and the authors show via the transfer-matrix diagonal that rerunning an attack on its own source model does not always succeed again, implying single-shot ASR estimates carry unreliability; (4) coding scenarios showed markedly lower ASR, plausibly because they were built from authentic coding-agent transcripts closer to models' safety-training distribution, while tool-use/computer-use scenarios used more LLM-simulated content -- an acknowledged realism gap the authors plan to address; (5) scenario counts are imbalanced across settings (18 tool-use vs 8 computer-use); (6) the study is single-turn/single-chance only and does not examine multi-turn escalation attacks; (7) built-in vendor guardrails/classifiers were explicitly disabled during testing, so results do not reflect production-deployed defense stacks (the authors ran a separate parallel competition for that); (8) the pass threshold of 7/10 for the prompt judge was carried over from a prior competition (ART) and 'was not empirically tuned on this dataset'; (9) GPT-5.1 was added only at the start of Wave 2, so its ASR/coverage numbers reflect less data than other models; (10) DeepSeek V3.1, Kimi K2, and SecAlign-70B lack vision support and were not tested on computer-use scenarios (29 vs 37 behaviors).

datasets_used: 41 curated agentic scenarios (18 tool-use, 15 coding, 8 computer-use) built for this competition; coding scenarios drawn from real repositories TRL, veRL, CrewAI plus authentic coding-agent transcript prefills
271,588 deduplicated attack attempts / 67,634 submissions / 8,648 successful attacks from the Gray Swan Arena competition (464 participants)
Curated benchmark of 2,679 successful attacks (up to 9 per model/behavior pair) used for transfer experiments and open-sourced (subset of 95 attacks against Qwen released publicly on Hugging Face)
GPQA Diamond scores (from Epoch AI, and from the Nova 2 technical report for Nova models) used as a capability proxy for correlation analysis
quantitative_results: ASR by agentic setting: Tool Use 4.82% [4.68%,4.96%], Computer Use 3.13% [2.96%,3.31%], Coding 2.51% [2.41%,2.62%]
Behavior coverage: Gemini 2.5 Pro and DeepSeek V3.1 100% (37/37 behaviors), Claude Opus 4.5 lowest at 35.1% (13/37)
Capability vs ASR correlation: r = -0.31, p = 0.3 (weak, not significant)

Transfer asymmetry: attacks that broke Claude Opus 4.5 (n=44) transferred at 44-81% to all other models; attacks from Claude Sonnet 4.5 transferred at 12-69%; attacks that broke Qwen3 VL 235B transferred at 0% to Opus 4.5 and 1% to Sonnet 4.5 while achieving 25-64% on other vulnerable models

Top 3 attack strategies overall (of 27): Fake Chain of Thought 4.3% ASR (13,878 submissions), Request to Disable Critical/Safety-Oriented Thoughts 4.1% (13,494), Offer Reward and Punishment 4.0% (6,575)
Top universal attack cluster ('Holodeck'): 63 submissions, effective across 21 of 41 behaviors and 9 models, mean intra-cluster cosine similarity 0.920

Judge-type breakdown: Gemini 2.5 Pro tool-judge pass rate ~16%, combined tool+prompt judge pass rate ~53%; Anthropic models' tool+prompt pass rates: Haiku 41.9%, Sonnet 37.1%, Opus 23.7%; Nova 2 Lite 37.8% vs Nova 1 Premier 33.2%
Kimi K2 thinking-enabled vs non-thinking transfer ASR: 30% vs 35%
Top 3 transfer strategies (~30% transfer ASR): User Provided Malicious Tool Call String, Fake Syntax and Delimiters, Fake User and Assistant Messages; Fake Chain of Thought transferred at 27%
baselines_compared: 13 frontier/open-weight models: Claude Opus 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Haiku 4.5 (Anthropic); GPT-5, GPT-5.1 (OpenAI); Gemini 2.5 Pro (Google, plus Gemini 3 Pro added only in transfer experiments); Grok 4 (xAI); Nova 1 Premier, Nova 2 Lite (Amazon); DeepSeek V3.1 (DeepSeek); Kimi K2 / Kimi K2 Thinking (Moonshot AI); Qwen3 VL 235B (Alibaba/Qwen); SecAlign-70B (Meta)

Prior agent-security benchmarks compared qualitatively in Table 1 for indirect-injection/concealment/tool/code/computer-use/update coverage: AgentDojo, InjecAgent, ART (prior), AgentHarm, OS-Harm, WASP, ASB, Agent-SafetyBench -- none of which combine indirect injection with a concealment requirement across all three agent settings
